signals.reasoning: BTC had been rebounding, but the fresh Hormuz blockade headlines and renewed veto make fresh directional exposure unattractive again. The event brake matters more than the local bounce.
ETH was participating in the rebound, but the fresh geopolitical shock resets the board back to risk management rather than new entries. Neutral is the disciplined call.
SOL had improved, but this is exactly the kind of headline regime where a clean-looking recovery can get interrupted. Better to stand down under veto.
STABLE still has squeeze fuel on paper, but the live event veto makes chasing it here a poor trade. Best raw long is not the same as best actual trade now.
WCT was interesting on squeeze mechanics, but execution quality remains messy and the fresh geopolitical shock makes it even less attractive. Watch-only at best.
GOAT is still damaged, but the downside is already too mature and unstable to trust as a fresh short. Weak structure alone is not enough.
VVV is still mostly a short-covering rebound rather than a clean durable long. Under veto, that is not enough to justify a fresh trade.
HEMI remains weak, but the structure is already too extended to chase on the short side, especially into a headline-driven environment. Better to leave it alone.
BLUR remains too mixed and too low quality to justify a new entry. The board has much higher event risk than edge right now.
JTO had real squeeze characteristics, but that is no longer enough to overcome the live veto. It moves back to watch-only instead of active long.
CHILLGUY is constructive, but already extended and too event-sensitive to chase. Under fresh geopolitical risk, that keeps it neutral.
AAVE had a healthy rebound, but the fresh headline shock takes it out of the actionable set. Better to avoid turning a decent chart into a bad event trade.
MEW is too thin and too unstable to trust through this kind of headline regime. No reason to force it.
GRASS has some squeeze ingredients, but still reads more like a short-covering bounce than a clean durable long. The veto pushes it back to neutral.
STBL is trend-supported and crowded, but not with a clean enough fresh setup to justify acting under renewed event risk. Not a good place to press.
DOT still has squeeze fuel, but the structure is mixed and the geopolitical backdrop makes chasing it unattractive. Better to stay flat.
AVNT remains more interesting than broken, but the edge is too small for a live veto environment. Watch-only, not actionable.
NIL is too thin and too neutral to matter here. No trade.
ALT is stabilizing, but not in a way that clears the bar for a fresh signal with headline risk elevated again. Neutral is the correct call.
DYM remains damaged, but even there the downside is already too advanced to trust as a fresh short into a veto regime. Better to stand down.
